title: Substance Use and Alcohol Withdrawal
subtitle: The nurse cares for a patient withdrawing from alcohol — using a nonjudgmental approach, giving thiamine before glucose, treating with scheduled assessment and benzodiazepines, and watching for seizures and delirium tremens.

patient.name: Alcohol Withdrawal
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely supports a patient in alcohol withdrawal.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient withdrawing from alcohol using a calm, nonjudgmental approach, treating substance use disorder as a chronic, treatable disease. Early withdrawal (about 6 to 24 hours after the last drink) brings tremors, anxiety, nausea, sweating, and autonomic arousal such as a fast heart rate, high blood pressure, and a low-grade fever. Withdrawal seizures can occur roughly 12 to 48 hours out, and delirium tremens (DTs) typically appear at 48 to 72 hours or later with confusion, agitation, hallucinations, and dangerously unstable vital signs; DTs are a medical emergency that can be fatal. A standardized scale such as CIWA-Ar guides symptom-triggered dosing. Benzodiazepines (for example lorazepam or diazepam) are first-line to ease withdrawal and prevent seizures and are tapered as symptoms settle. Thiamine (vitamin B1) is given, and importantly before any glucose, to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy; folate and fluids are also provided. The nurse maintains seizure and fall precautions, provides a calm, quiet, well-lit environment, and reorients the patient frequently. Vital signs and mental status are monitored closely. As the patient stabilizes, the nurse reinforces hope, avoids shaming, and connects the patient to counseling, recovery programs, and ongoing support.
providerOrders: Use a calm, nonjudgmental approach and treat SUD as a disease
Assess withdrawal severity on a schedule (e.g., CIWA-Ar)
Give benzodiazepines as ordered to ease withdrawal and prevent seizures
Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Maintain seizure and fall precautions and monitor for delirium tremens

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about alcohol withdrawal?

opts.text: Early signs include tremor, anxiety, and sweating
Withdrawal seizures can occur in the first 1-2 days
Delirium tremens is a medical emergency
Substance use disorder should be met with shaming
Tachycardia and hypertension are common
Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment
Thiamine is given before glucose
A scale like CIWA-Ar guides dosing
Bright, loud stimulation calms the patient
Seizure and fall precautions are needed
DTs include confusion and hallucinations
Frequent reorientation helps a confused patient

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order admitting a patient in early alcohol withdrawal

opts.text: Assess vital signs and withdrawal severity
Institute seizure and fall precautions
Give thiamine before any glucose
Give benzodiazepines per the scale/order
Reassess and document the response
Mild hand tremor and anxiety at 8 hours
Severe confusion and vivid hallucinations at day 3
Nausea and sweating soon after the last drink
Dangerously high heart rate, fever, and disorientation
Feeling jittery and craving alcohol
Agitation with unstable vital signs needing emergency care

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: On day 3, a patient is confused, hallucinating, and has HR 130 and fever
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize delirium tremens and ensure safety
Stay with the patient and reduce stimulation
Notify the provider urgently
Give benzodiazepines as ordered
Monitor vitals and mental status closely
Giving thiamine before glucose
Shaming the patient for drinking
Maintaining seizure precautions
Placing the patient in a bright, noisy room
Using CIWA-Ar to guide benzodiazepine dosing
Ignoring rising heart rate and confusion
Reorienting and reassuring the patient
Referring to counseling and recovery support

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
